content.problemStatement:
There is only 111 TV in the common room, and as luck would have it, both the El Clasico football match and the India-Pakistan cricket match are happening simultaneously.

There is one football fan who is looking at the live commentary online and will scream whenever an 'important' event takes place in the El Clasico. Similarly, there is a cricket fan who will do so for every important event in the cricket match.

You are given two arrays - F1,F2,…,FnF_1, F_2, \ldots, F_nF1​,F2​,…,Fn​, which denote the times when an important event happens in the football match. And similarly C1,C2,…,CmC_1, C_2, \ldots, C_mC1​,C2​,…,Cm​ for cricket.
You sadly have the remote in hand. You start out by watching the El Clasico. But whenever an Important event happens in the sport which isn't on the TV right now, you will be forced to switch to that sport's channel, and this continues, i.e., you switch channels if and only if when an important event in the other sport happens.
Find the total number of times you will have to switch between the channels.

Input:
First line will contain TTT, number of testcases. Then the testcases follow.
Each testcase contains of 333 lines of input.
First line contain 222 space separated integers NNN and MMM, number of important events in football and cricket match respectively.
Second line contain NNN space separated integers FiF_iFi​, where the ithi^{th}ith index represents ithi^{th}ith important event in the football match.
Third line contain MMM space separated integers CiC_iCi​, where the ithi^{th}ith index represents ithi^{th}ith important event in the cricket match.
Output:For each testcase, output in a single line answer to the problem.
constraints: Fi < Fi+1 for all valid i
Ci < Ci+1 for all valid i
Fi != Cj for any i, j
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of three lines: the first line contains two space‑separated integers N and M, the number of important events in the football and cricket matches respectively. The second line contains N space‑separated integers F1 … FN in strictly increasing order – the times of football events. The third line contains M space‑separated integers C1 … CM in strictly increasing order – the times of cricket events. All times are distinct across the two lists.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the total number of times you will have to switch the TV channel.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: merged order 1(F),2(C),3(F),4(C). Start on football, switches at 2,3,4 → 3. Test case 2: merged order 1(C),100(F),200(F),300(F). Starting on football, the first event is cricket, so one switch to cricket, then at 100 a football event occurs causing a second switch back to football. No further switches. Total 2.
Events: 5(F) – no switch (already on football), 10(C) – different sport → one switch.
Chronological order: 100(F) – no switch, 150(C) – switch 1, 200(F) – switch 2.

solution.approach: Both event lists are already sorted and contain distinct times. We merge the two lists in chronological order using two pointers. While merging we keep track of the channel currently being watched (0 for football, 1 for cricket). The TV starts on the football channel. Whenever the next event belongs to a sport different from the current channel we increment the switch counter and update the current channel. This count equals the number of times the sport changes in the merged sequence, plus a possible initial switch if the first event is cricket. The algorithm runs in O(N+M) per test case and uses O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N + M) per test case, O(total N + total M) for the whole input.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the input arrays.
